claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment. 

First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:

**How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** are generally not eligible

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we'll go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm inquiring about my two children actually. Alex is 11 and Blake is 15.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you with your Child Benefit eligibility for both Alex (11) and Blake (15).

Since both of your children are under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. However, I need to check a few more things to confirm eligibility.

Let me check the next requirement:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake working 24 hours or more per week in paid work?**

(In other words, are they both either not working at all, or working less than 24 hours per week?)

This requirement applies to children aged 16 and over, but I need to confirm for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work that much. Alex doesn't work at all, and Blake doesn't either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Neither Alex nor Blake are working 24 hours or more per week, so that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, I'm asking if either child receives:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits claimed in their own name

Most children don't receive these, so this is usually straightforward.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake in an apprenticeship program?**

This is important because certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility:
- **English apprenticeships** (Intermediate, Advanced, Higher, Degree) would disqualify
- **Scottish Modern Apprenticeships** would disqualify
- **Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships** are approved and don't disqualify
- **Other Welsh apprenticeships** would disqualify

Are either of your children in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in any apprenticeship programs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check **your residency status** (as the person claiming Child Benefit).

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If you normally live in the UK, that's straightforward. However, if you don't, there are other ways you might still qualify, such as:
- Having settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Having pre-settled status with certain conditions (working, studying, sufficient resources, etc.)
- Being a Crown servant living abroad

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I live abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since you live abroad, I need to check if you meet any of the special circumstances that would still allow you to claim Child Benefit.

**Which of the following applies to your situation?**

1. **Crown servant living abroad** - Are you (or your partner) a Crown servant working abroad for the UK government?

2. **EU Settlement Scheme status** - Do you have either:
   - Settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
   - Pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?

3. **Living in certain qualifying countries** - Are you living in specific countries that have agreements with the UK (such as certain EEA countries or Gibraltar)?

4. **None of the above** - You don't meet any of these circumstances

Which situation best describes you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: None of those apply to me, I'm afraid. I'm just living abroad and don't have any of those special circumstances.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Unfortunately, this means you don't meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Decision

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

### Summary for Each Child:

**Alex (age 11):**
- ❌ **Not Eligible**
- Alex meets all the child-related requirements (correct age, not working full-time, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship)
- However, you cannot claim because you don't meet the residency requirements

**Blake (age 15):**
- ❌ **Not Eligible**  
- Blake meets all the child-related requirements (correct age, not working full-time, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship)
- However, you cannot claim because you don't meet the residency requirements

### Why You're Not Eligible:

To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet special circumstances such as:
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
  - Being a Crown servant working abroad
  - Living in certain qualifying countries with agreements with the UK

Since you live abroad and don't meet any of these special circumstances, you unfortunately cannot claim Child Benefit.

### What You Could Consider:

- If your circumstances change and you move back to the UK to live normally, you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that time
- If you gain settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme in the future, this may also open up eligibility

I understand this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions about this decision or your situation changes, please don't hesitate to reach out again.

reasonings: Alex is 11 years old and meets the child-related criteria (under 16, not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship). However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they live abroad and do not have settled status, pre-settled status, Crown servant status, or residence in a qualifying country. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old and meets the child-related criteria (under 16, not working 24+ hours per week, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship). However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they live abroad and do not have settled status, pre-settled status, Crown servant status, or residence in a qualifying country. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children, Alex (11) and Blake (15), who both meet all the child-related eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. However, the claimant lives abroad and does not meet any of the special residency circumstances required to claim Child Benefit from outside the UK (such as being a Crown servant, having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or living in certain qualifying countries). As residency requirements are mandatory for the claimant, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for either child.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
